## Key Points

- If all current staff and programs are carried forward with inflation, the district faces an 18.95% tax increase; the board has set a 6% ceiling, creating a $7.2 million gap that must be closed
- Declining enrollment is the primary driver: South Portland is at its lowest enrollment level in a decade, mirroring a statewide trend of fewer K-12 students with rising costs
- Staffing levels have continued to rise even as enrollment declined, creating a fundamental math problem; student needs are increasingly complex requiring more intensive programs
- Three "big ideas" proposed for cost savings: (1) elementary school reconfiguration by grade band (~$2M savings), (2) review schedules/staffing at grades 5-12 for efficiencies (~$1M), (3) shift changes and operational efficiencies (~$1M) -- but even all three together leave a $3M+ gap
- Elementary reconfiguration would shift from geographic to grade-band schools (e.g., pre-K-2 schools and 3-4 schools), moving average class sizes from ~16 up to 18-19 (K-2) and 22-23 (grades 2-4), still within board guidelines
- Consolidating middle school bell times was removed from proposals because it requires permitting and DOT traffic studies that cannot be completed by September 2026; may be viable for fall 2027
- The fund balance (savings account) has been depleted through prior-year use, removing a cushion that previously helped offset tax burden
- Health insurance is budgeted at 12% increase (up from earlier 8% estimate) based on signals from peer districts budgeting 10-15% increases; actual figure not known until late March
- School closing was not proposed because the district needs to take on pre-K services for 3-5 year olds under new state mandates and move toward universal pre-K
- A superintendent search is underway alongside the budget process; the community was urged to participate in stakeholder input sessions
